coredata.dc:title: Electrospun polylactic acid/ tungsten oxide/ amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (PLA/WO3/N-CQDs) fibers for oil/water separation and photocatalytic decolorization
coredata.dc:creator: Nugraha, Muhammad Wahyu
Wirzal, Mohd Dzul Hakim
Ali, Fathilah
Roza, Liszulfah
Sambudi, Nonni Soraya
coredata.dc:description: Oily wastewater generated from various industrial processes and oil spillages is a massive threat to the environment and human health. Hence, it is essential to develop novel material for practical oil/water separation and recovery. By incorporating WO3 (tungsten oxide)/ N-CQDs (amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots) into the matrix of polymer, the alteration of wettability behavior of electrospun PLA fiber is reported in this present work. The modified PLA/WO3/N-CQDs fibers shows a significantly enhanced hydrophobicity of the fibers while maintaining surface super-oleophilicity. These behaviors are achieved by modifying the surface contact angle and surface morphology of the composite PLA fiber using WO3/N-CQDs as filler. The PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA fiber exhibits the highest water contact angle (WCA) at 132.37º, oil absorption capacity at 35.752 g/g for n-hexane, oil separation performance at 8,326.048 L m−2 h−1 for n-hexane and 11,961.364 L m−2 h−1 for n-heptane, and decolorization of methylene blue (MB) at 91.80%. Additionally, the PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA fiber demonstrated an excellent separation performance and durability after ten times cyclic separation performance test. This present study contributes to a potential application in the field of oily wastewater treatment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oily wastewater generated from various industrial processes and oil spillages is a massive threat to the environment and human health. Hence, it is essential to develop novel material for practical oil/water separation and recovery. By incorporating WO3 (tungsten oxide)/ N-CQDs (amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots) into the matrix of polymer, the alteration of wettability behavior of electrospun PLA fiber is reported in this present work. The modified PLA/WO3/N-CQDs fibers shows a significantly enhanced hydrophobicity of the fibers while maintaining surface super-oleophilicity. These behaviors are achieved by modifying the surface contact angle and surface morphology of the composite PLA fiber using WO3/N-CQDs as filler. The PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA fiber exhibits the highest water contact angle (WCA) at 132.37º, oil absorption capacity at 35.752 g/g for n-hexane, oil separation performance at 8,326.048 L m−2 h−1 for n-hexane and 11,961.364 L m−2 h−1 for n-heptane, and decolorization of methylene blue (MB) at 91.80%. Additionally, the PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA fiber demonstrated an excellent separation performance and durability after ten times cyclic separation performance test. This present study contributes to a potential application in the field of oily wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: The oily wastewater can be originated from various industrial processes such as metals processing, automotive, food, petrochemical, and oil & gas [1,2]. Statistically, massive volumes of oily wastewater with oil concentrations ranging from 50 to 1000 ppm have been generated [3]. About nearly 1.7–8.8 metric tonnes (MT) of oils per year are discharged to the oceans [4]. This waste is highly hazardous because it contains organic compounds i.e. aliphatic hydrocarbons, aromatic hydrocarbons, compounds containing NSO (nitrogen/sulphur/oxygen), and asphaltene [5]. This ecologically hazardous oily wastewater should be treated before being discharged due to environmental regulations. The current tolerance limit of total oil and grease concentrations in wastewater ranges from 5 to 40 ppm [2]. Oily wastewater can cause severe long-term effects. It can be contaminating drinking water and groundwater resources, endangering aquatic resources and human health, affecting crop production, and destructing the natural landscape [6].

Functional materials with excellent absorption capacity are highly required to effectively treat oily wastewater. In recent years, the super-oleophilic and hydrophobic materials with capability to selectively absorbing oil from water have been developed extensively. Many studies have embarked on materials with superwettability, e.g., metal mesh, freestanding filter film, aerogel, sponges, manganese nanoparticles, and textiles [7–10]. As one of the biodegradable materials, PLA, has been successfully developed into novel biodegradable separation materials [11]. A previous study from Gu et al. using commercial non-woven PLA modified with hydrophobic material SiO2 (silica dioxide) successfully alters the wettability behavior of the PLA. PLA/PS (polystyrene) /SiO2 composite, demonstrated superhydrophobic property with water contact angle (WCA) of 152.0 ± 2.1°[12]. Additionally, Shi et al. study demonstrates good oil/water separation and photodegradation of modified commercial non-woven PLA/TiO2 (titanium dioxide) composite [13]. Despite the development of materials for oily wastewater treatment, it is essential to create new functional surfaces with high versatility to effectively treat oily wastewater. One of the promising improvements was by incorporating fillers into the PLA. The metal oxide nanoparticle as filler has advantages such as nontoxicity, high chemical stability and ability to improve separation performance by altering the surface wettability and photodegradation [14–16]. N-CQDs is one of the nanomaterial fillers that can alter surface wettability, and its abundant amino functional groups have high selectivity towards cationic dye molecules for photodegradation purpose [17,18]. The amino-functionalization strategy was widely utilized for adsorbent for oil-saltwater separation, CO2 capture, Cu(II) removal, dye removal i.e., MB, neutral red, basic fuchsin, Remazol brilliant blue, congo red, methyl violet, methyl orange, rhodamine B and p-nitrophenol [19–21]. Liu et al. work used EDTA impregnated CO2 adsorbent to achieve 95.2% of adsorption capacity [22]. Moreover, a study by Xing used NH2-rich poly(allylamine hydrochloride)-graphene oxide nanosheet (PAH-GO) for Cu(II) removal with excellent adsorption capacity at 349.04 mg/g [23]. In addition to that, the work from Fan et al. shows a significant adsorption capacity of 676.22 mg/g for methylene blue using N- functionalized magnetic chitosan and graphene oxide (MCGO) [24]. Hence, the combination of CQDs characteristics and the effect of N-functionalization will be beneficial for the composite adsorbent. To achieve the effectivity of oil/water separation and simultaneously photodegradation the organic pollutants, WO3/N-CQDs is incorporated into PLA matrix. The combination of WO3 and CQDs shows satisfying performance for photodegradation of rhodamine B, methyl orange, phenol, and tetracycline hydrochloride [25–27]. However, it is yet to be tested as nanofiller for oil/water separation.

In this present study, the fabricated PLA fiber is modified with WO3 and N-CQDs to alter the fiber wettability behavior. The super-oleophilic and hydrophobic composite was fabricated and exhibited excellent oil absorption capacity, oil separation performance, and photocatalytic performance for decolorization of MB. Hence, the as-prepared PLA/WO3/N-CQDs fibers show a high potential for water treatment applications.

Polylactic acid (PLA) (IngeoTM biopolymer 3251D) was purchased from NatureWorks LLC (USA). Sugarcane bagasse was locally purchased in Malaysia (Seri Iskandar, Perak). Chloroform (CAS 67–66–3) was purchased from R&M Chemicals (Malaysia) N, N-Dimethylformamide anhydrous, 99.8% (DMF) (CAS 68–12–2), Hexadecane (CAS 110–82–7), n-hexane (CAS 110–54–3), n-heptane (CAS 142–82–5), sodium hydroxide pellets (CAS 1310–73–2), EDA (CAS 107–15–3), and Titriplex® III (ethylenedinitrilotetraacetic acid (EDTA)) (CAS 6381–92–6) were purchased from Merck (Germany). Sodium tungstate dihydrate ≥ 99% (CAS 10213–10–2) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA), and hydrochloric acid (CAS 7647–01–0) was purchased from Fischer (USA). MB was purchased from Bendosen (Malaysia). These chemicals were employed without further purification or treatment. De-ionized (DI) water (18.2 MΩ) from PureLab Flex was utilized for the whole experiment.

The preparation of tungsten oxide was performed using the precipitation method from the previous study [28]. The 200 mL of 15 mM sodium tungstate dihydrate was prepared, then 80 mL of hydrochloric acid (HCl) was employed dropwise into the solution for 1 h at 5–10 ºC with constant stirring. Afterwards, the solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm to separate the precipitate, and the precipitate was then washed until the pH reached 6. The precipitate was added into 250 mL DI water, and underwent ultrasonication for 2 h. The solution was kept at 48 h at room temperature for the crystallization process. Subsequently, the solution was vacuum filtrated using a 0.45 µm PVDF membrane and precipitate was dried at room temperature for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CQDs was prepared using a hydrothermal method from the previous study [29] using sugarcane bagasse as a carbon source with some modifications. The sugarcane bagasse was burned in a muffle furnace for 1 h at 600 ℃. Then, the 300 mg obtained biochar was added into 30 mL of 0.5 M sodium hydroxide under constant stirring. Solutions of 1% w/w EDTA and 2.5% v/v EDA were added separately to provide functionalization on CQDs. Afterwards, the solution was heated at 190 ℃ for 24 h. The solution was then cooled to room temperature and resulted N-CQDs solution was vacuum filtered using 0.45 µm PVDF membrane. The N-CQDs solution was then dialyzed using a dialysis membrane (MWCO3500) overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of polylactic acid/ tungsten oxide/ amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (PLA/WO3/N-CQDs) fibers
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the WO3/N-CQDs were prepared by mixing WO3 with 1 wt% N-CQDs, then the solution was stirred for 2 h before dried overnight at 60 °C to obtain powder of WO3/N-CQDs. Secondly, 3:1 v/v chloroform to DMF solution was prepared and ultrasonicated for 30 min before the PLA pellets were added into the mixture to form 20 wt% PLA solution. The different proportions of blank, 1 wt% WO3, 1 wt% WO3/N-CQDs EDTA, and 1 wt% WO3/N-CQDs EDA were added into PLA solution. Subsequently, the solution was stirred for 24 h at room temperature. The various PLA, PLA/WO3, and PLA/WO3/N-CQDs solutions were electrospun (NLI SP 20, Nanolab Instruments Sdn. Bhd., Malaysia) using 10 kV applied voltage, 1.5 mL/h flowrates, 15 cm distance between needle tip and collector with 400 rpm rotation speed for the collector drum. The schematic diagram of the fibers fabrication process is presented in Fig. 1.

The as-prepared samples were cut into 2.5 × 2.5 cm square pieces. Then, various organic solvents of n-hexane, n-heptane, and hexadecane were selected as the oil components to be added into the water, which was colored by MB to distinguish the different liquids. The absorption test was performed at room temperature. The sample was immersed into the oil/water mixture for 3 min. Then, the soaked fibers were removed from the mixture, drained in the air for 30 s, and then weighed. The absorption capacity was calculated as following Eq. (1) [30].

A mixture of each 50 mL of organic solvents and water (dyed with MB) was poured into the filtration setup, and the solution was filtered through the as-prepared PLA, PLA/WO3, and PLA/WO3/N-CQDs fibers. Subsequently, the separation efficiency of as-prepared fibers was determined using Eq. (2) as follow [31].

Where J (L m−2 h−1) is volumetric flux, V (m3) is volume of the permeate, A (m2) is effective area of the fiber, and t (h) is the filtration time. The full setup of the equipment is presented in Fig. 2(b) and Supplementary Video S1.

The decolorization performance was investigated using MB dye solution (5 ppm, 100 mL), where 0.6 g/L fibers were added into the solution. The setup consists of a batch reactor with a magnetic stirrer, two halogen lamps (each 80 watts) (Philips, USA) as a light source, and a cooling fan. The full setup of decolorization performance can be seen in Fig. 2(c). Before the lamp was turned on, the mixture of composite and MB dye was stirred vigorously and kept in the dark for 30 min. At a certain time interval, 3 mL of sample was withdrawn and subsequently centrifuged to remove the remaining fiber. The absorbance of samples was measured using UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Spectroquant Prove 600 (Merck, Germany), λ = 664 nm) to determine the MB dye concentration. The decolorization efficiency of MB dye can be expressed in the following Eq. (3):

Surface morphology imaging and elemental analysis were investigated using Zeiss Supra 55 VP FESEM/ EDX (Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy/ Energy-dispersive X-ray) and Universal Scanning Probe Microscope (USPM) using Nano Navi (E-Sweep), respectively. The contact angle of the fibers was analyzed using goniometer Rame-Hart 260. The functional groups were characterized using Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy– attenuated total reflectance (FTIR–ATR) Perkin Elmer Spectrum One, without any additional preparation. X-Ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (XPS) analysis was done using Thermo Scientific K-Alpha. The crystallinity of samples was analyzed using an X-ray Diffractometer (XRD) on X′Pert3 Powder and Empyrean PANalytical with Cu Kα irradiation (λ = 1.54) range (diffraction angles (2θ)) from 5° to 80° with a step size of 2°/step and exposure time of 1 s/step. The optical properties were analyzed using UV-Vis DRS Agilent Cary 100.

The oil/ water separation performance is mainly dependent on surface wettability. The pristine PLA and three surface modified PLA fibers (PLA/WO3, PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA, and PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDTA) were tested using water and n-hexane to emulates the oil. Fig. 3(a) illustrates the as prepared fibers behavior towards water and oil. Moreover, oil contact angle (OCA) is also investigated. The results show all four as-prepared fibers (pristine PLA, PLA/WO3, PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA, and PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDTA) OCAs at 0º contact angle as the oil contacted the surface of the fiber, the contact angle was recorded instantaneous within 3–5 s of the droplet. Therefore, all as-prepared fibers show super-oleophilic behavior. The previous study using only PLA also exhibited excellent superoleophilicity capability [11].

The water contact angle (WCA) measurement is shown in Fig. 3(b). The same as previous determination of OCAs, the WCAs was recorded instantaneous within 3–5 s of droplet. The pristine PLA exhibits WCA of 113.70º, showing a hydrophobic behavior. After the modification with tungsten oxide, the as-prepared PLA/WO3 exhibits higher WCA at 121.81º. The notably increased WCA is the contribution of WO3, which its incorporation enhanced the hydrophobicity of fibers. This surface modification behavior shows similar agreement from previous studies, which surface coating of WO3 [32] and WO3 thin film [33] enhanced hydrophobicity. Moreover, the addition of metal oxide and hydrophobic filler of TiO2/SiO2 in PLA, which is presented in Ye et al. study shows an increase in hydrophobicity up to 28.57% [15]. This trend is also exhibited in hydrophobic GQDs (graphene quantum dots) from Zhu et al. study, using cross-linked coal-based graphene quantum dots into electrospun nanofibers. The fibers demonstrated WCA up to 144º after the introduction of hydrophobic GQDs into the composite [17].

Furthermore, the addition of WO3/N-CQDs into the PLA fibers further increased the hydrophobicity of fibers. The WCA increased to 123.33º for PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDTA and 132.37º for PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA. This behavior can be attributed to the chemistry of the surface. As can be seen in Fig. 5, after the addition of WO3/N-CQDs, the characteristic vibration peaks such as -OH and H-O-H sharply decreased. The capped in a hydrophilic group of the fibers is also consequential to the increased water contact angle [34,35].

The fibers have been characterized with FESEM to investigate the morphology of the fibers. It can be seen from Fig. 4, that the diameter of fibers samples are 1.125 ± 0.153 µm, 2.946 ± 0.965 µm, 8.149 ± 0.494 µm, and 1.848 ± 0.328 µm for pristine PLA, PLA/WO3, PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDTA, and PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA, respectively. The larger diameter distribution can be attributed to the addition of WO3 and WO3/N-CQDs nanoparticles as fillers, which reduce the surface energy and stabilize the solvent solution [36]. The fibers are shown to have pore formation. The most influential attribute that affects pore formation is phase separation process. Solvent-rich regions transformed into pores as the solvent dries out during the electrospinning. This fast-pace solvent evaporation is most likely the reason for the pore formation. However, the nanoparticles could absorb the solvent and slow down the evaporation process, which decreased the surface energy in the polymer solution. This ultimately decreased the evaporation rate [37]. Porous fibers could provide more attachment points for organic pollutants, hence the photodegradation efficiency could increase as well [38].

Furthermore, the AFM analysis was also conducted to determine the surface topography of the fiber. Fig. 5 shows 3D morphologies of the PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA fiber. The surface of PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA fiber shows obvious valleys and ridges. The average mean square roughness (Ra) is exhibited at 25.72 nm within the area of 0.25 µm2. The surface roughness of the as-prepared modified PLA, PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA is shown to have higher surface roughness compared to the previous study using modified PLA fiber. The study by Krasian et al. using PLA and tungsten disulfide blend (PLA/WS2) and PLA, molybdenum disulfide, and tungsten disulfide blend (PLA/WS2-MoS2) exhibited Ra of 0.351 and 0.416 µm within area of 50 × 50 µm2, respectively [39].

The functional groups of fibers are shown in Fig. 6. The characteristic absorption band of pristine WO3 at 710 cm−1 (peaks a) and 1006 cm−1 (peaks b) are attributed to W–O stretching mode, and WO bond, respectively[40]. Moreover, pristine PLA fibers absorption bands are depicted at 1130 cm−1 (peak c) and 1185 cm−1 (peak d) spectra regions which ascribed to the stretching vibration of C–O–C and C–CH3 (represented the backbone ester group from PLA), respectively [41,42]. While peak e and peak f at absorption band 1390 cm−1 and 1750 cm−1 display the characteristic of −OH and H−O−H of the interstitial water molecule, respectively [34,43]. Lastly, peak g at absorption band at 3000–3600 cm−1 shows O—H stretching vibrations which only pristine WO3 exhibited. This indicates the hydration nature of pristine WO3 [44].

After incorporating WO3 into the pristine PLA, there is no apparent change of WO3 stretching band in the composite fibers. This is due to the relatively small amount of WO3 added into the fiber. Similarly, the addition of WO3/N-CQDs composite didn’t show any – NH2 groups because of the weak molecular interaction between the N-CQDs and PLA [41]. However, from Fig. A.1, we can see the peak difference after addition of N-CQDs solution into pristine WO3. The peak c at absorption band at 1500 cm−1 corresponds to NH2 [45,46]. We can see an apparent slight increase in that peak. The N-CQDs EDA has the most increasing in peak intensity because of the higher percentage of EDA added into pristine WO3. However, the vibrations of -NH2 cannot be detected in the composite fibers in Fig. 6, due to small loading amount. The addition of pristine WO3 increases intensity of peaks e and f because of its hydration nature of the as-prepared WO3. Furthermore, after the addition of WO3/N-CQDs, the intensity of peak f significantly decreased. This indicates that the amination reaction has been successfully ensued and resulting in more hydrophobic fibers [35].

XPS analysis was performed to identify the chemical composition change due to the incorporation of WO3/N-CQDs into the PLA fibers. The quantitative XPS analysis ( Fig. 7) shows XPS spectra C 1s and O 1s core level spectra of PLA; W 4f, C 1s, and O 1s core level spectra of PLA/WO3; W 4f, C 1s, and O 1s core level spectra of PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDTA; W 4f, C 1s, and O 1s core level spectra of PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA. However, EDX did not detect any N element. This can happen because of low amount of existing nitrogen to be measured [47]. Similar result is shown in previous study by Babu et al. using N-doped TiO2, which at 1% of N-doped, the XPS could not reveal that N is presence [48]. The full summary of the chemical composition of XPS analysis is presented in Table 1.

The XRD spectra of samples are shown in Fig. 8. The pristine WO3 peaks exhibited at 11.3º, 23.7º, 25.2º, 30.4º are attributed to WO3 monoclinic phase (JCPDS No. 18–1420) and WO3 cubic phase (JCPDS No. 054–0508). Subsequently, the XRD diffraction peaks analysis of pristine PLA fiber shows a broad hump at 2θ of 10–20°. This indicates the amorphous nature of PLA fiber [49]. The three as-prepared surface modified PLA fibers exhibit a strong diffraction peak around 11.3º, and various peaks between 23º-27º correspond to the monoclinic phase of WO3 [50]. The interaction of PLA and N-CQDs in WO3 changes the crystalline structure of WO3. The XRD pattern peaks of PLA/WO3, PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDTA, and PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA show good agreement with JCPDS No. 00–051–2411 pattern peaks attributed to monoclinic tungsten hydroxide. The N-CQDs diffraction peaks cannot be detected since the amorphous nature of the carbon and the small amount of N-CQDs.

The optical properties effect of WO3/N-CQDs towards PLA fibers was also evaluated using UV–vis DRS absorption spectra illustrated in Fig. 9. As we can see from the figure, pristine PLA shows weak absorption in the 250–700 nm region. After the addition of pristine WO3 and WO3/N-CQDs EDTA, the absorption was extended to 200–470 nm. The WO3 and WO3/N-CQDs EDTA extends the absorption in the UV region (200−400) and slightly in the visible light region (400−470). This can be associated with the WO3 bandgap, which varies around 3.2 eV (386 nm) to 2.4 eV (515 nm) [51]. Furthermore, WO3/N-CQDs EDA extends the absorption region of fibers to 200–700 nm. The extended light absorption of fiber also occurred due to the addition of N-CQDs. N-CQDs, which has strong photoluminescence emission, functioned as a matrix in the semiconductor WO3 that ultimately improved the light absorption of the PLA fibers [52]. In addition to that, the bandgap energy estimation of each fiber was also conducted [53]. From Fig. A.2, it can be seen that the bandgap energy of pristine PLA decreased after the addition of WO3 and WO3/N-CQDs. The estimated bandgap energy are 4.88, 4.83, 4.44, and 4.27 eV for pristine PLA, PLA/WO3, PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDTA, and PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA, respectively.

The oil absorption of each sample is investigated using three different organic solvents e.g. n-heptane, n-hexane, and hexadecane. Fig. 10 displays the summary of the oil absorption performance. The porous structure of the pristine PLA exhibits good performance of organic solvents absorption at 18.94, 19.11, and 18.54 g/g for n-heptane, n-hexane, and hexadecane, respectively. The PLA/WO3 exhibits slightly better performance than the pristine PLA. This is because of the higher hydrophobicity of PLA/WO3, which improved the oil absorption performance [32]. Furthermore, the PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA shows the best performance of organic solvents absorption at 27.90, 35.72, and 32.51 g/g for n-heptane, n-hexane, and hexadecane, respectively, which has the most hydrophobic characteristic among all fibers. This results show good agreement with previous study which favors the oil absorption on hydrophobic surface [12,13].

The separation performance of PLA/WO3/N-CQDs fiber was carried out using three different organic solvents and shown in Fig. 11. The separation performance is increased in the order of pristine PLA, PLA/WO3, PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDTA, and PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA. The higher flux performance is the effect of wettability behavior of the fibers, with PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA as the most hydrophobic fibers exhibited the highest flux. The enhancement of flux can be increased up-to 2-folds when the WO3/N-CQDs EDA was embedded in fibers. The previous literatures demonstrate similar trends. Study by Ye et al. reported the incorporation of hydrophobic nanoparticles of SiO2. It shows the addition of SiO2 increased the hydrophobicity of the fiber and increased flux performance [15]. Generally, the flux performance can be increased up to three times using hydrophobic nanomaterials [1,4].

Further evaluation is the recycle test of the best performed sample as exhibited in Fig. 12, using the n-hexane/water mixture. After each cycle, the as-prepared sample is rinsed with deionized water to remove the oil droplets on the fiber surface, afterwards the fiber was wiped with tissue paper to remove deionized water from the surface before next recycle process. The PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA fiber demonstrated an excellent flux even after multiple cycles. The recycles affected the fiber performance at only around 3.31%. Thus, the as-prepared PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA fiber presented high durability. The same trend of excellent recycling performance was observed from the previous study. The PLA/PS/SiO2 fiber from Gu et al. study reveals a 3–6° change in contact angle and approximately around 9.13% flux difference after ten cycles [54].

The catalytic decolorization of samples is observed by using MB dye. The investigation of blank MB sample decolorization was carried out first to determine the self-removal effect of MB towards light irradiation. It is found that the self-removal MB contributes to only around 9–10% towards the decolorization performance of fibers. From Fig. 13, it can be determined that the incorporation of WO3 and N-CQDs significantly enhanced the decolorization of MB. The decolorization performances are increased in the order of pristine PLA with 60.18% removal, PLA/WO3 with 86.52%, PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDTA with 88.73%, and the best removal was achieved by using PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA fibers at around 91.80%. Additionally, the performance of fiber sample was also tested using various concentrations of MB dye. The PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA fiber was used because the composite showed the best decolorization performance. From Fig. 14, at higher initial MB concentrations, the performance of fiber decreased. It shows that fiber can decolorize 91.80%, 70.43%, 66.67%, and 35.71% of 5 ppm, 10 ppm, 15 ppm, and 50 ppm MB, respectively. Furthermore, the determination of stability of sample towards decolorization of MB was also performed using PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA fiber to decolorize 5 ppm MB for three cycles. The sample fiber from the previous cycle was collected and then washed with DI water and dried at 60ºC before used for the next cycle. As we can see from Fig. 15, the performance of fiber was drastically decreased after the third recycle with only 26.07% removal, while after the second recycle shows relatively good performance with 62.85% removal.

Additionally, the turnover number (TON) and turnover frequency (TOF) of the composites were calculated and presented in Table 2. TON indicates the number of moles pollutant degraded per gram of photocatalyst, which estimate the decolorization reactivity [55]. The results demonstrate that PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA fiber exhibits the highest TON, which in agreement with the decolorization performance. The higher TON and TOF indicate higher reactivity and stability of the sample for longer reaction time, respectively. However, TON and TOF are not intrinsic to photodegradation because photodegradation depends on light intensity. Therefore, it can only be compared when the operating conditions were exactly similar [56].

Furthermore, the kinetic model of the decolorization was developed to determine the kinetic rate. The photocatalytic decolorization process occurred in heterogeneous phase, which fitted into linear and non-linear pseudo-second-order listed in Eqs. (6) and (7) as follows [57–59]:

Where the qe and qt represent the adsorption capacities at equilibrium and at time t, respectively, and k2 represents pseudo-second-order rate constant (g/mg. min). As seen in Fig. A.3, the data of t/qt vs. time were plotted to determine the kinetic rate of linear pseudo-second-order. From the fitting, we can determine that the composite is governed by pseudo-second-order kinetics. This indicates that the adsorption process of MB occurs due to the chemisorption [60,61]. Additionally, the kinetic data shown an increased constant rate of PLA composite compared to pristine PLA, and the highest constant rate is found for PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA. The rate constants and regression coefficient of each sample are presented in Table A.1. Furthermore, the non-linear form of pseudo-second-order was determined by Microsoft Excel Solver which has been used in the previous study [62]. The results are shown in Fig. A.4 and Table A.2 that qe values are closed to the experimental data.

There are two main factors for the decolorization performance of the PLA fiber. One is attributed to the special wettability of PLA fiber, which has enlarged the effective contact area with the water-soluble organic matters. Another is due to the photodegradation property of WO3 nanoparticles [12,13]. Besides the abundant binding sites of WO3 as adsorbent and its photodegradation ability of the organic compounds, the N-CQDs elevated the selectivity of MB cationic dye due to electrostatic interaction between the amino groups of the dye molecules [18,63]. Study by Elsayed et al. shows the increased adsorption performance of sodium alginate/polyvinylpyrrolidone beads after tungsten oxide were embedded into the composite. It shows the increased removal up to 98% for WO3 nanorods structure embedded into the beads composite [64]. Moreover, the effects of CQDs incorporated into WO3 nanocomposites also exhibited excellent performance. Study by Yan et al. using WO3/CQDs nanocomposite demonstrated 100% MB removal after 30 min contact time, and study by Smrithi et al. using snake gourds-derived CQDs/WO3 shows 88% removal efficiency of cadmium ions [63,65]. Additionally, study by Bibi et al. using amino-functionalized-MOFs (metal organic frameworks) shows 99% efficiency, and the functionalization decreases the equilibrium contact time from 120 min to only 30 min [18]. In that regards, WO3/N-CQDs ultimately enhanced the PLA fiber selectivity and decolorization performance.

The results of the as-prepared PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA sample are compared with other materials from previous studies. Table 3 depicts the oil/water separation properties (flux and absorption capacity) and the photodegradation capability of different materials. It is shown that PLA/WO3/N-CQDs demonstrates satisfying performance of oil absorption and separation with high photodegradation performance. Hence, the material shows high potential to be utilized for oil/water separation and photocatalytic applications.

In this present work, PLA/WO3/N-CQDs fibers was successfully synthesized by a facile electrospinning method. The incorporation of WO3/N-CQDs alters the wettability and surface morphology of the PLA fiber, which ultimately enhanced the hydrophobicity which contributes to super-oleophilic behavior. The contact angle of as-prepared PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA fiber is greatly increased up-to 132.37º compared to the pristine PLA fiber at 113.7º. The absorption capacity of fiber was increased by approximately ~87.05%, oil /water separation and decolorization performance are enhanced by approximately ~112.11% and ~31.62%, respectively, compared to pristine PLA fiber. Furthermore, The PLA/WO3/N-CQDs EDA fiber exhibits a stable separation performance after being reused for ten cycles. The PLA/WO3/N-CQDs fibers may provide a solution for oily wastewater treatment.
